publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2021-07-10 18:15:32 UTC.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Cornell Cinema records
did.abstract: The Cornell Cinema video collection includes U.S. and international art film and art video works and documentary, as well as video distribution catalogs. As director of Cornell Cinema from 1982-1994, Richard Herskowitz began a program of purchasing and lending film and video artworks on videocassette. Subsequent Cinema director Mary Fessenden continued this program as long as demand persisted, then donated the video collection to the Rose Goldsen Archive of New Media Art in 2014. The collection is an important part of the history of Cornell Cinema. The collection focuses on significant works of historical, international, independent art cinema and video art. It includes catalogs from two video art exhibitions and annual catalogs from video distributors of the 1980s and 1990s. It also has Cornell Cinema calendars and posters from 1971 to 1983.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524rareref@cornell.eduhttp://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: ORGANIZATIONAL HISTORY
bioghist.p: Cornell Cinema was founded in 1970 and has earned an international reputation as a university film series and also a media center dedicated to providing the Cornell community with experimental, avant-garde, independent, and mainstream media art and cinematic entertainment. With nearly 300 screenings each year, Cornell Cinema has been cited as one of the best campus film exhibition programs in the United States.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: The Cornell Cinema video collection includes U.S. and international art film and art video works and documentary, as well as video distribution catalogs. As director of Cornell Cinema from 1982-1994, Richard Herskowitz began a program of purchasing and lending film and video artworks on videocassette. Subsequent Cinema director Mary Fessenden continued this program as long as demand persisted, then donated the video collection to the Rose Goldsen Archive of New Media Art in 2014. The collection is an important part of the history of Cornell Cinema. The collection focuses on significant works of historical, international, independent art cinema and video art. It includes catalogs from two video art exhibitions and annual catalogs from video distributors of the 1980s and 1990s. It also has Cornell Cinema calendars and posters from 1971 to 1983.

arrangement.head: SERIES LIST
arrangement.p: Series II. Printed Materials Box 12, Mapcase Folders 1-6

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Cornell Cinema records, #37-7-4187. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

userestrict.head: Restrictions on Use:
userestrict.p: Due to the fragility and potential degradation of moving image and sound recordings, viewing and listening is limited to items that have been digitized. If an item is in another media format, you may request to have the item digitized for access. Information on ordering access copies may be found on our Reproductions and Permissions page.

I Do Not Know What it is I am Like - Bill Viola Length: 1:29:00 Format: VHS
Video Americana Length: 56:00 Format: VHS
SABDA - Daniel Reeves Length: 30:00 Format: U-Matic S
Love part I (recut) Format: U-Matic
Love part II (recut) Format: U-Matic
Joan Does Dynasty with Joan Braderman Length: 32:00 Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hampshire College professor and stand-up theorist Joan Braderman takes on power and desire in TV's campiest series. "Joan Does Dynasty," a classic in feminist media deconstruction, is a hilarious look at the materialistic 80's in the context of the ordinary working person's life. Co-Director Manual Delanda. Screened at the Whitney Museum of American Art (2007) and New Museum of Contemporary Art in NYC (2008).

did.unittitle: Fighting the Fundamentalist Fig Leaf Length: 29:00 Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Mapplethorpe/Serrano controversies sparked a pressing debate on censorship of the arts. Artist David Avalos, Washington Project for the Arts' Philip Brookman, critic Coco Fusco, poet Allen Ginsberg and painter Leon Golub discuss the issues at stake. What is the conservative agenda behind arts censorship and what can we do to combat it?

did.unittitle: Manhatta. Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Charles Sheeler and Paul Strand. Manhatta documents the look of early-20th-century Manhattan. With the city as subject, the film consists of 65 shots sequenced in a loose non-narrative structure, beginning with a ferry approaching Manhattan and ending with a sunset view from a skyscraper. The primary objective of the film is to explore the relationship between photography and film; camera movement is kept to a minimum, as is incidental motion within each shot. Each frame provides a view of the city that has been carefully arranged into abstract compositions.

did.unittitle: My Mother Married Wilbur Stump - Skip Sweeney Length: 28:00 Format: U-Matic
Catherine Saalfield, Neil Lazarus and Kelly Anderson Read the Weekly Mail Length: 28:00 Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Analysis of an anti-apartheid South African newspaper with anti-censorship strategies and a non-racial social democratic vision.

Torn Between Colors: Youth and the Media Length: 1:00:00 Format: U-matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Through Our Eyes Video and History Project - Satellite Academy High School Bronx.

did.unittitle: Transformer/AIDS with Bob Kinney Length: 28:00 Format: U-matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: At the end of his second term, Ronald Reagan finally addressed the issue of AIDS. Or did he? Certainly not without the homophobia, insensitivity and blatant lack of knowledge which plagues the whole of government around such issues. "Transformer AIDS" looks at the governmental response to AIDS at this crucial point in history, when activism forced the issue onto the public agenda. A sarcastically funny, yet poignant critique engineered spearheaded by critic Bob Kinney.

did.unittitle: A Simple Case for Torture, OR How to Sleep at Night Length: 62:00 Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Martha Rosler identifies the totalitarian implications of an argument for torture, under certain circumstances, as it appears as a guest editorial in Newsweek magazine in 1982. Her critique is presented as a voiceover and a dizzying assemblage of radio and print media--articles on subjects ranging from human rights to unemployment and global economics. Implicating the U.S. government and American businesses for supporting regimes that systematically use torture, she indicts the American press for its role as an agent of disinformation through selective coverage and its use of language, and for implicitly legitimizing points of view that support torture.

Herman Slobbe/Blind Child 2 - Johan van der Keuken Length: 60:00 Format: U-Matic
# 1 Reading Lesson; The Door; Filmmakers Holiday Length: 60:00 Format: U-Matic
Animals in the Mass Media: Images of a Schizophrenic Society - John Sanbonmastu Format: U-Matic
Ynestra King Reads Seventeen Length: 28:00 Format: U-Matic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Activist and Ecofeminist Ynestra King reads Seventeen magazine. Ynestra exposes how the publication's superficial and commercial based message to teen girls leaves them without the necessary tools for identity based empowerment and can ultimately isolate special needs and minority teen groups. Ms. King also outlines numerous non-commercial methods for improving this publication such as addressing much more common women's issues like community health resources, sexuality counseling and social awareness.

Series II. Printed Materials
Uris Library Media Department Videos and Films
Video distribution catalogs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes distributors Electonic Arts Intermix, Picture Start, Video Data Bank, Frameline Distribution, The Kitchen, The Video Project, Museum of Modern Art

did.unittitle: Video distribution catalogs - zine format

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes distributors Paper Tiger Television, Art Com, One Way Films, Drift Distribution, Video Out, Alternative Filmworks

did.unittitle: Exhibition catalogs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Catalogs of media art exhibitions co-sponsored by Cornell Cinema and the Johnson Museum: Media Buff: Media Art of Buffalo, NY (1988), and Border Crossings: The Cinema of Johan van den Keuken (1990). 2 copies of each.

did.unittitle: Cinema ticket
Calendars and Posters

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Folders are chronologically organized by calendar year, rather than Cornell's academic year.
